coredata.dc:title: Identification of cell chemistries in lithium-ion batteries: Improving the assessment for recycling and second-life
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wett, Christopher
Lampe, Jörg
Görick, Dominik
Seeger, Thomas
Turan, Bugra
coredata.dc:description: Recycling and second life of lithium-ion batteries are vital for lowering the growing resource demand of sectors like mobility or home energy storage. However, an often-overlooked issue is the sometimes-unknown cell chemistry of batteries entering the end-of-life. In this work, a machine learning based approach for the identification of lithium-ion battery cathode chemistries is presented. First, an initial measurement boundary determination is introduced. Using the Python Battery Mathematical Modelling (PyBaMM) framework, synthetical partial open circuit voltage (OCV) charge and discharge curves are generated with an electrochemical single particle model for three different cathode chemistries and the initial state of charge and state of health values as well as the initial capacities are varied. The dV/dQ characteristics are chosen as features and four machine learning algorithms are trained on different lengths of OCV curves. The trade-off between achievable accuracy and the number of OCV steps showed that an increasing accuracy correlates with a higher step number. While extremely small charge and discharge capacities per step did not yield sufficient testing accuracies, capacities starting from 0.2 Ah per step up to 0.6 Ah per step showed increasingly good results with an accuracy of up to 89.3 % for 0.5 Ah and 15 OCV steps. Additionally, the approach was validated by classifying experimental data. The results especially demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach to distinguish between lithium iron phosphate (LFP) and lithium nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Second-life
Recycling
Cell chemistry
Identification
Machine learning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Feasible voltage zones for the cell chemistry identification of unknown lithium-ion batteries where UB is the upper voltage boundary and LB is the lower voltage boundary.
Flowchart for determining the cell chemistry identification test boundaries with respective handling decisions and downstream usability diagnosis.

Flow chart of the data driven approach including data generation, training of machine learning algorithms, and testing on synthetical and experimental data on the example of discharging. (a) Varying max. lithium concentration (red lines) and initial SOCs (blue dots). (b) Exemplary generated discharge GITT curves with different starting SOCs. (c) Set of OCV curves from an exemplary data set. (d) Exemplary dV/dQ feature curves. (e) Confusion matrices as performance metrics for different ML models.
Confusion matrix for experimental test cases with 5 and 10 discharge steps and 0.3 A with 0.1 h relaxation and 0.25 A with 1 h relaxation respectively.
Highest accuracies reached for discharge case with varying ∆Q values from 0.05 Ah to 0.6 Ah for 5, 10, and 15 steps and best performing classifier.
Highest accuracies reached for charge case with varying ∆Q values from 0.05 Ah to 0.6 Ah for 5, 10, and 15 steps and best performing classifier.
Accuracies of LFP/NMC classification for 10 OCV steps at 0.25 A and 1 h relaxation and edge case with 5 OCV steps at 0.3 A and 0.1 h relaxation.
Precision, Recall, and F1 score for the experimental validation cases as displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 4.

head.ce:title: Identification of cell chemistries in lithium-ion batteries: Improving the assessment for recycling and second-life

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rheinische Hochschule Köln University of Applied Sciences, Vogelsanger Str. 295, 50825, Cologne, North-Rhine-Westphalia, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rheinische Hochschule Köln University of Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aRheinische Hochschule Köln University of Applied Sciences, Vogelsanger Str. 295, 50825, Cologne, North-Rhine-Westphalia, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Siegen, Institute of Engineering Thermodynamics, Paul-Bonatz-Str. 9-11, 57076, Siegen, North-Rhine-Westphalia, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bUniversity of Siegen, Institute of Engineering Thermodynamics, Paul-Bonatz-Str. 9-11, 57076, Siegen, North-Rhine-Westphalia, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recycling and second life of lithium-ion batteries are vital for lowering the growing resource demand of sectors like mobility or home energy storage. However, an often-overlooked issue is the sometimes-unknown cell chemistry of batteries entering the end-of-life. In this work, a machine learning based approach for the identification of lithium-ion battery cathode chemistries is presented. First, an initial measurement boundary determination is introduced. Using the Python Battery Mathematical Modelling (PyBaMM) framework, synthetical partial open circuit voltage (OCV) charge and discharge curves are generated with an electrochemical single particle model for three different cathode chemistries and the initial state of charge and state of health values as well as the initial capacities are varied. The dV/dQ characteristics are chosen as features and four machine learning algorithms are trained on different lengths of OCV curves. The trade-off between achievable accuracy and the number of OCV steps showed that an increasing accuracy correlates with a higher step number. While extremely small charge and discharge capacities per step did not yield sufficient testing accuracies, capacities starting from 0.2 Ah per step up to 0.6 Ah per step showed increasingly good results with an accuracy of up to 89.3 % for 0.5 Ah and 15 OCV steps. Additionally, the approach was validated by classifying experimental data. The results especially demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach to distinguish between lithium iron phosphate (LFP) and lithium nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With a rapidly growing application of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles (EVs), second-life usage, recycling processes, and safe handling become increasingly important. Car manufacturers use different types of LIBs for their EV traction batteries with varying casings, sizes, and cell chemistries. These characteristics play an important role especially for the second-life usability and possible recycling paths [1].

When it comes to assessing the relevant states e.g., for the usability of aged traction batteries in second-life applications, it is crucial to determine indicators like the state of health (SOH), state of power (SOP), and identifiable defect and aging mechanisms like lithium plating, soft short-circuits, or solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth [2–5]. However, nowadays recycling companies also face the issue that often there is no reliable knowledge about the chemical properties, i.e., the cell chemistry, of each battery [6]. As there is no mandatory regulation in place for cell and pack manufacturers to provide specific information about the chemistry, this issue will gain relevance with the rising number of EVs on worldwide markets [7]. Without any knowledge of the material composition particularly of the cathode, the process of state estimation becomes difficult due to unknown boundaries of a cell, module or pack. With no information about voltage limits, the battery may be accidentally overcharged or deep discharged when applying a current excitation. Furthermore, knowledge of the chemical composition of batteries is of high economical interest for recycling companies, as the values of the used materials vary significantly for different cathode chemistries [8]. Zhou et al. [9] highlight the relevance of cathode recycling by developing a data driven methodology to accelerate the discovery of deep eutectic solvents. Currently, sorting in the pre-treatment process for battery recycling is only carried out in certain recycling procedures [7]. Additionally, current sorting techniques primarily focus on e.g., the casings and labels of battery packs or cells and are not able to classify unknown batteries. Dobó et al. [7] therefore conclude that there is the need for a classification of batteries based on their electrode chemistry to enhance the effectiveness of the recycling process. Neumann et al. [10] also identify a demand for a better separation of the different lithium-ion battery compositions to increase recycling and re-use rates. The current focus in recycling lies on the recovery of cobalt but needs to be expanded to other elements [7]. Another main focus in the battery recycling processes is the total time required [1]. It is critical to ensure a time efficient handling and identification of usability of used cells to enable higher re-use rates and concurrently sustain the economic viability [5].

Hence, identifying the cell chemistry of an unknown battery or battery system is crucial for moving towards higher re-use rates of LIBs and for increasing the economic benefit of recycling processes. While there are several studies on the characterization of lithium-ion battery components after the dismantling procedure, cf. [11] and [12], the aim is to be able to identify the batteries beforehand to enable second-life applications and classify the recyclability and safety of the battery. Up to now, no such procedure has been presented in the literature.

In this work, a machine learning (ML) approach is proposed to distinguish cell chemistries based on partial open circuit voltage curves generated by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT). Beforehand, a decision algorithm is introduced for determining the measurement boundaries, since state of charge, state of health, and nominal capacities are unknown. This is especially important as existing SOC and SOH estimation approaches assume the battery to be in a feasible voltage range for testing, as the battery type is known [13]. For the training of a robust ML algorithm, a large amount of training data is necessary [14]. As the identification of cell chemistries should be independent of the aging stage of the battery, the training data provided to the algorithm needs to represent OCV curves of differently aged cells. Since gathering enough data for each chemistry experimentally could be time-consuming, the data sets used in this work are generated synthetically by using an electrochemical single particle model (SPM) in the PyBaMM modelling framework [15]. Thereby, large data sets can be created with specifically chosen degradation states by varying relevant electrochemical parameters such that they depict real world aging or degradation mechanisms.

The chosen parameters are used for generating training data sets for each cell chemistry based on PyBaMM parameter sets. These data sets are then used for training different ML algorithms that are evaluated regarding their accuracy. The algorithms are validated by identifying the cell chemistries of synthetically generated test data, as well as experimentally gathered partial OCV curves from different lithium-ion batteries with varying cathode compositions.

When analyzing a battery with an unknown cell chemistry, the test boundaries are mainly determined by the cathode composition of the cell. For example, an LFP cell is usually safely operatable in a voltage range between 2.0 V and 3.6 V. An NMC cell on the other hand generally allows for cycling between 2.5 V and 4.2 V. Hence, before the chemistry identification itself can take place, the testing boundaries need to be specified. For this reason, a simple procedure is introduced, where actual cell voltages can be classified into green, yellow, orange, and red zones. Each color refers to a voltage zone and its feasibility for testing.

When investigating an unknown battery with a related nominal voltage range
[
V
m i n
,
V
m a x
]
, the nominal safely operatable voltage region (green zone) is given by
[
max
V
m i n
, min
V
m a x
]
, considering the maximum and minimum of lower and upper voltage limits of different cell chemistries.

To allow for situations where the cell voltage is slightly above
V
m a x
or slightly below
V
m i n
, which are also typically considered as safely operatable [16], the following applicable voltage range can be defined as the green zone by adding a tolerance of 0.2 V to the upper and lower boundary.
(1)
[
max
V
m i n
− 0.2
V , min
V
m a x
+ 0.2
V
]
.

The proposed tolerance value of ± 0.2 V is meant as a suggestion and can be adjusted if necessary. For the considered case here, the nominal safe region is [2.5 V, 3.6 V] and the safely operatable region [2.3 V, 3.8 V].

Then, a further voltage range can be defined, where a safe operation might still be possible, given the right cell chemistry (yellow zone). Again, assuming a slight over- or undercharge to be acceptable yields
(2)
[
min
V
m i n
− 0.2
V , max
V
m a x
+ 0.2
V
]
,
which is [1.8 V, 4.4 V] for the investigated case of LFP and NMC (with
min
V
m i n
= 2
V
a n d
max
V
m a x
= 4.2 V
). Here, cell chemistry identification can still be carried out as LFP cells are usually still safely operatable even at a voltage above 4 V [17] and deep discharging only leads to severe degradation mechanisms but not to hazardous failure [18]. The voltage range below the yellow zone is denoted as orange zone, as it does not lead to dangerous failure but irreversibly damages the battery. Consequently, batteries in the orange zone are only considered for recycling. The region above the upper yellow zone is denoted as red zone, as significant overcharging can lead to hazardous failures [19]. In the case considered, all voltages below 1.8 V belong to the orange zone and all voltages above 4.4 V belong to the red zone.

If
V
c e l l
is in the red zone, safe handling is required. If a cell is identified as being overcharged after an initial classification into the yellow zone, again safe handling is required. Here, this could be the case for e.g., an LFP cell at 4.3 V.
If a cell is identified as being in the orange zone after an initial classification into the yellow zone, again recycling is required. Here, this could be the case for e.g., an NMC cell at 1.9 V.

If
V
c e l l
is located in the green or yellow zone, it has to be determined if the cell chemistry identification is performed via discharging or charging, by comparing the distance to the corresponding interval border:
(3)
V
c e l l
−
(
max
V
m i n
− 0.2
V
)
≥
(
min
V
m a x
+ 0.2
V
)
−
V
c e l l
→
I d e n t i f i c a t i o n
b y
d i s c h a r g i n g
(4)
V
c e l l
−
(
max
V
m i n
− 0.2
V
)
<
(
min
V
m a x
+ 0.2
V
)
−
V
c e l l
→
I d e n t i f i c a t i o n
b y
c h a r g i n g
with the corresponding maximum safe discharge or charging ranges of
Δ
V
d i s c h a r g e
=
V
c e l l
−
(
max
V
m i n
− 0.2
V
)
and
Δ
V
c h a r g e
=
(
min
V
m a x
+ 0.2
V
)
−
V
c e l l
respectively. In the investigated case for e.g., a cell voltage of
V
c e l l
= 2
V
this would result in a cell chemistry identification by charging up to an upper limit of 3.8 V within a voltage range of
Δ
V
c h a r g e
= 1.8
V
. In case of
V
c e l l
= 3.4
V
this would result in an identification by discharging to at most 2.3 V and a corresponding voltage range of only
Δ
V
d i s c h a r g e
= 1.1
V
. The worst case for identification purposes would be a cell voltage exactly in the middle of the interval, i.e., which is given by
(5)
V
c e l l
=
min
V
m a x
− max
V
m i n
2
.

In the considered case, this is
V
c e l l
= 3.05
V
with a voltage range of only
Δ
V = ± 0.75
V
for charging and discharging respectively. Fig. 1 depicts the suggested voltage ranges.

With the gathered information, the partial OCV curves can be measured. To ensure that all lithium-ion cells can be tested regardless of their cathode chemistry, it is necessary to limit the applied current to generally small values. By fixing the discharge or charge time and currents, the discharged or charged capacities are limited. Here, we consider the 18650 format which usually limits the maximum capacity to around 4 Ah and the minimum capacity to 1.2 Ah [20]. Thus, it should be possible to charge or discharge a total capacity equal to at least 0.6 Ah, i.e., half of the assumed minimum capacity of 1.2 Ah. However, some aspects must be considered:
•
LFP cells have a large voltage plateau in the mid-SOC range. This makes it difficult to estimate the battery SOC based on a voltage measurement.
•
Capacity fade in degraded batteries potentially reduces the minimum capacity of 1.2 Ah.
•
The lower voltage limit of NMC batteries can sometimes be higher than 2.5 V [21,22]. This may also reduce the safely dischargeable capacity.

As a consequence, the trade-off between small capacity (dis-)charge steps and the accuracy of the cell chemistry identification is determined in this work. The number of OCV steps is set to 5, 10, and 15 for comparison.

Note that lithium titanate oxide batteries are neglected as they are not relevant in terms of their numbers on the second-life market of batteries [23]. In addition to that, including them would lead to a less broad voltage range in which the batteries could be cycled as the voltage range of these batteries is usually between 1.5 V and 3.0 V.

The complete decision process is depicted in Fig. 2 . In a first step, the temperature of the battery is measured to ensure that no thermal hazard is present. If the cell temperature is significantly higher than the ambient temperature (
Δ
>
5 ∘
C
), safe handling is required, and the battery cannot be considered for second-life applications and recycling may be limited or only possible following pretreatment. After that, the previously explained determination of the feasible measurement range is conducted after the cell voltage is measured. Then, the cell chemistry identification can be carried out. Finally, with the knowledge of the cathode composition, a usability diagnosis regarding the suitability of the battery for second life or recycling can be executed [5].

To train the ML classifiers, various data sets are necessary to cover all desired test cases and to determine the trade-off between charged or discharged capacity
Δ
Q
and the achievable classification accuracy. To obtain data that enables the classifiers to become robust and accurate, data of differently aged cells with different starting SOCs is necessary. Synthetical data was generated by using an electrochemical SPM from PyBaMM [15]. The model based on the work of Marquis et al. was tested against more complex electrochemical models and showed good simulation results under different current loads while performing best at smaller current rates [24]. Additionally, cell degradation was modeled by varying the maximum lithium concentration in the active material of both the positive and negative electrode. While other parameters like SEI thickness, temperature, and diffusion coefficients in the active material were also varied, the maximum lithium concentration proved to be the most suitable parameter to easily include capacity degradation in the used SPM among the tested variables. The maximum lithium concentration is directly impacted by a capacity fade due to the loss of active material and has also been shown to be an effective parameter to simulate aging mechanisms in electrochemical models by extensive sensitivity analyses like [25] or [26]. A flow chart of the data driven approach is depicted in Fig. 3 . Three parameter sets were used for data generation. The first one contains parameters for an NMC532 cell taken from the work of Mohtat et al. [21]. The second one contains parameters for an NMC811 cell taken from Chen et al. [27]. Finally, the third parameter set describes an LFP cell taken from Prada et al. [28]. One data set is necessary for each tested incremental capacity per step
Δ
Q
. Each set contains 250 individual OCV curves for each chemistry, meaning that each data set consists of 750 OCV curves. The SOH values are discrete binned values between 80 % and 100 % by varying the maximum lithium concentrations of both electrodes. The starting SOCs are uniformly distributed random values between 50 % and 100 % SOC for discharge and 0 % to 50 % SOC for charge as shown in Fig. 3 (a).

To ensure that the cathode chemistries are not identified by their different cell capacities, the electrode geometries are adjusted to three different capacities for training purposes, i.e., to 1.2 Ah, 2 Ah, and 3.5 Ah, covering the common capacity range of 18650 cells. For this reason, the thickness of the positive and negative electrodes is modified accordingly.

As illustrated in Fig. 3 (b), the simulations were then carried out using 5, 10, and 15 discharge and charge steps of 0.5 h with equal discharge and charge capacities per step and with 1 h relaxation time between pulses. For a total discharge or charge amount of 0.6 Ah and 15 steps, this corresponds to a discharge or charge capacity per step of
0.6
A h
15
= 0.04
A h
. This capacity is discharged or charged within 0.5 h with a related current of
0.04
A h
0.5
h
= 80
m A
, followed by 1 h relaxation time. In this case, the total required time results in
15 * (
1 + 0.5
h
) = 22.5
h
. Accordingly, the total testing time is 15 h for 10 steps and 7.5 h for 5 steps. The discharge step was aborted if the cell dropped below or exceeded the set voltage limits. If this condition was not reached, the simulation was terminated after all steps were finished. Afterwards, the OCV points were extracted from the simulation data as shown in Fig. 3 (c). Note that in order to ensure robustness, a gaussian random noise with zero mean and a standard deviation of 0.001 was applied to the generated voltage data.

In a next step, the dV/dQ features are calculated from the raw voltage and charged or discharged capacity data (Fig. 3 (d)). The differential voltage data is used here, as it has been proven to be an efficient feature for data driven approaches, e.g., for SOH estimation [14]. A common 80 %/20 % train-test split is carried out and the features are scaled using standardization [29]. Other common train-test splits did not yield significantly different results.

Four algorithms often used in the context of energy systems were chosen for training, to test different approaches and their performance [9,30]:
•
Support vector classifier (SVC)
•
Decision tree (DT)
•
Random forest (RF)
•
Logistic regression (LR)

While multinomial logistic regression is a model of low complexity that works particularly well for linearly separable data, SVC represents a more sophisticated approach, that is generally better suited for non-linear data. Decision trees on the other hand provide the advantage of interpretability as concrete classification criteria that can be directly deduced from the trained model. Random forest represents an ensemble approach that lowers the likeliness of overfitting that comes along with decision trees. Note that the classifiers could also be connected to a combined approach to gain improved results, which is not considered here. Ultimately, the inference on test and experimental validation data was carried out and performance metrics of the trained models were compared (see Fig. 3 (e)).

Intuitively, one might expect that capturing more characteristics of the OCV curve would lead to greater accuracy in classification. Therefore, several different charge and discharge capacities per step were tested and the step number was varied between 5, 10, and 15 steps. Table 1 lists the achieved test accuracies and the best performing classifier for each case.

It can be observed that the accuracies do not show a monotonic relation to the discharged capacities. However, when leaving out the edge case with
Δ
Q = 0.05
A h
, a clear relation between the number of steps and the achieved accuracies can be observed. All variations lead to a performance that is higher than a completely randomly guessing classifier. A crucial performance measure for the classifier is the distinction between LFP and NMC cells as explained in Section 1. The corresponding accuracies and confusion matrices are included in the supplementary material. Table 2 displays the accuracies for the charge case and additionally specifies the best performing classifier. The overall accuracy improves with the number of steps but is also not monotonic. The same holds for ascending charge capacities. Again, the edge case with
Δ
Q = 0.05
A h
does not match the overall behavior. This indicates that 0.05 Ah is too low to yield robust results for both charge and discharge case. These results suggest that for the observed charge and discharge ranges, the resolution of the curves, i.e., the step number, is of higher importance than the total charge amount. However, this only holds for the desired ranges, as experiments with larger charge amounts up to the full cell capacity led to 100 % accuracy for the test data. Smaller charge amounts led to a decrease in accuracy for all step numbers. The non-monotonic behavior may also be influenced by different starting SOCs leading to varying information content within each snippet of the OCV curve.

Out of 18 test cases for discharge, the RF classifier performed best for 13 cases, followed by SVC (2) and LR (2), and DT (1), clearly indicating an advantage of the RF classifier. This aligns with the best performing classifiers for the charge case in Table 2 (RF: 14, SVC: 3, DT: 0, LR: 1).

To practically demonstrate the developed approach, two experiments were carried out on commercial cells. The first experiment was conducted with 10 OCV steps and a discharge current of 0.25 A (1 h relaxation time in-between steps, 0.5 h discharge step duration). A second experiment was carried out as an edge case scenario with only 5 OCV steps, 0.3 A discharge current, and a reduced relaxation time of only 0.1 h while maintaining a discharge step duration of 0.5 h Thus, e.g., for the 5-step case the total discharged capacity is
0.5
h · 0.3
A · 5 = 0.75
A h .
The advantage of the latter one is a significant speed-up of the measurement process and thus an acceleration of the classification. All cells were tested starting from an SOC of 100 % down to 0 %. The resulting OCV curves were then separated into 5-step and 10-step OCV curves accordingly, each covering a unique SOC span.

The following commercial cells were used:
•
IFR 18650, 1.4 Ah (cathode chemistry: LFP)
•
LG INR18650 MJ1, 3.5 Ah (cathode chemistry: NMC811)
•
Samsung INR18650-20R, 2.0 Ah (cathode chemistry: NMC532)

Finally, the classification of LFP and NMC was carried out, as for the practical application the distinction between LFP and NMC is most critical. As shown in Table 3 , for 5 steps an 86.5 % accuracy was reached for 37 samples, whereas for 10 steps, an accuracy of 84.9 % was achieved for a total of 33 samples.

The confusion matrices for both experimental cases are depicted in Fig. 4 . The highest accuracies for the 5-step case were reached for the NMC identification where 19 out of 20 samples of NMC811 and 10 out of 11 samples of NMC532 were classified correctly by SVC. For the LFP cell, 3 out of 6 samples were classified correctly by RF.

For the 10-step case, the NMC identification was again high with 7 out of 8 correct classifications for NMC532, and 18 out of 19 correct classifications for NMC811 by RF. The LFP identification again reached 3 out of 6 correctly classified samples by LR.

As the focus of the experimental validation was to showcase the performance of the approach in distinguishing between LFP and NMC cells, further metrics are evaluated for the results of Table 3. For binary classification tasks, three metrics are commonly used: Precision, recall, and the F1 score. The resulting values are shown in Table 4 . Particularly the high recall values for NMC batteries indicate that the model is sensitive for correctly identifying most NMC cells. As a misclassification of NMC cells would be the economical worst-case scenario, these results are promising regarding a real-world application of the method. However, the rather low values for LFP cells in accordance with the accuracies shown before indicate that future work should focus on improving the LFP classification especially considering the sensitivity of the approach.

In this work, an approach was introduced to identify the cathode chemistry of lithium-ion batteries for application in recycling and second life. First, the initial determination of test boundaries was introduced. An electrochemical single particle model was used to create synthetical data of partial open circuit voltage curves for two NMC cells and one LFP cell. Four different machine learning algorithms were trained and tested which have proven the suitability of the approach. Afterwards, experimental validation was conducted. The results for synthetical data showed a correlation between the number of OCV steps and the reached accuracy, while the experimental validation yielded comparable accuracies. As the main goal for real-world application is the correct distinction between LFP and NMC cathode chemistries, the accuracy for this differentiation was also assessed, demonstrating the effectiveness of the developed methodology.

The presented approach was primarily motivated by the need for feasible procedures to identify unknown cells with unknown chemistry, SOC, and SOH, which is a prerequisite for a successful diagnosis of batteries regarding their recycling and second-life usability. The presented results indicate the practicability of the approach. However, several improvements are necessary for implementation in real-world recycling processes. E.g., the duration of measurements needs to be reduced. A measurement with 5 OCV steps of 0.5 h each and with a relaxation time of 1 h takes up 7.5 h Shortening the relaxation time to 0.1 h leads to a total testing time of 3 h To further improve the identification speed, approaches like partial charge/discharge curves [14] and pulsing methods can be applied, that have already been successfully used for SOH estimation and diagnosis purposes [31,32].

To enable an online cell chemistry identification with changing lengths of OCV curves, the approach needs to be expanded by, e.g., using a long short-term memory (LSTM) approach that can handle different sizes of inputs [33].

Finally, for practical usage further training needs to be conducted. This includes the creation of more data sets at additional SOH and SOC states, the inclusion of cells with different cathode compositions and overall different cell chemistries, and an extended variation of initial cell capacities. As the developed methodology possesses a broad applicability, future work should also include other relevant cathode mixtures like nickel cobalt aluminum oxides (NCA) and even beyond lithium-ion technologies like sodium-ion batteries.

Christopher Wett: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jörg Lampe: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Dominik Görick: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Thomas Seeger: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Bugra Turan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action (SUSTAIN, 16BZF320B).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.egyai.2024.100468.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recycling lithium-ion batteries from electric vehicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Harper et al., “Recycling lithium-ion batteries from electric vehicles,” Nature, vol. 575, no. 7781, pp. 75–86, Nov. 2019, doi: 10.1038/s41586-019-1682-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calculation of the state of safety (SOS) for lithium ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Cabrera-Castillo, F. Niedermeier, and A. Jossen, “Calculation of the state of safety (SOS) for lithium ion batteries,” J Power Sources, vol. 324, pp. 509–520, Aug. 2016, doi: 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2016.05.068.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-board soft short circuit fault diagnosis of lithium-ion battery packs for electric vehicles using extended Kalman filter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: “On-board soft short circuit fault diagnosis of lithium-ion battery packs for electric vehicles using extended Kalman filter,” CSEE JPES, 2020, doi: 10.17775/CSEEJPES.2020.03260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Model-based state of charge and peak power capability joint estimation of lithium-ion battery in plug-in hybrid electric vehicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Xiong, H. He, F. Sun, X. Liu, and Z. Liu, “Model-based state of charge and peak power capability joint estimation of lithium-ion battery in plug-in hybrid electric vehicles,” J Power Sources, vol. 229, pp. 159–169, May 2013, doi: 10.1016/j.jpowsour.2012.12.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the state of usability for lithium–ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wett, J. Lampe, J. Haß, T. Seeger, and B. Turan, “On the state of usability for lithium–ion batteries,” Batteries, vol. 10, no. 2, p. 57, Feb. 2024, doi: 10.3390/batteries10020057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: End-of-life or second-life options for retired electric vehicle batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu et al., “End-of-life or second-life options for retired electric vehicle batteries,” Cell Rep Phys Sci, vol. 2, no. 8, p. 100537, Aug. 2021, doi: 10.1016/j.xcrp.2021.100537.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Dobó, T. Dinh, and T. Kulcsár, “A review on recycling of spent lithium-ion batteries,” Energy Rep, vol. 9, pp. 6362–6395, Dec. 2023, doi: 10.1016/j.egyr.2023.05.264.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mohr, J.F. Peters, M. Baumann, and M. Weil, “Toward a cell-chemistry specific life cycle assessment of lithium-ion battery recycling processes,” J Ind Ecol, vol. 24, no. 6, pp. 1310–1322, Dec. 2020, doi: 10.1111/jiec.13021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Machine learning models accelerate deep eutectic solvent discovery for the recycling of lithium-ion battery cathodes

Rudolph, “Automated mineralogy as a novel approach for the compositional and textural characterization of spent lithium-ion batteries,” Miner Eng, vol. 169, p. 106924, Aug. 2021, doi: 10.1016/j.mineng.2021.106924.
